title: Elson.ai
description: A local-first macOS voice agent for dictation, cleanup, replies, and paste-first desktop work.
body:
Elson.ai is the voice layer I want on my own Mac: fast dictation, transcript cleanup, screenshot-aware replies, and a paste-first workflow that ends inside the app I am already using.

It is intentionally local-first and inspectable. Not another black-box assistant window. Elson sits closer to the desktop, listens when needed, cleans up the messy human input, and gives me text that is ready to use.

## What it does

- Turns speech into usable writing for chat, docs, email, and daily desktop work.
- Cleans rough transcripts into text that sounds like a person, not a recorder dump.
- Uses screenshot context for lightweight replies and better situational help.
- Keeps the flow paste-first so the assistant does not become the workspace.
- Treats the Mac as the real surface: permissions, app context, clipboard flow, and fast correction matter more than another chat tab.

## Recent work

The recent focus has been making Elson feel less like a demo and more like a daily utility. That means boring product work: cleaner installer and desktop flows, tighter prompt behavior, legacy chat fixes, and fewer rough edges between speech input and usable output.

There was also infrastructure work around where Elson-related projects live and how they can be inspected from the build machine. The public point is simple: the project is being moved from a local experiment toward a repeatable product workflow, with code, app surface, and deployment context easier to reason about.

## What changed in the story

The page now gives Elson more room as a desktop product, not just a voice demo. The important loop is speech in, cleaned text out, paste into the app you were already using. That keeps the assistant useful without forcing the user to move their work into the assistant.

## Why it matters

Voice is one of the fastest inputs we have, but most voice tools still feel bolted on. Elson is the version that feels closer to how I actually work: local, quick, editable, and open enough to improve in public.

## Public boundary

The public version is the product direction and desktop workflow. It does not expose private local files, credentials, internal app data, direct messages, or unreleased operational details.
